summary: McKenzie discovers that a librarian's email newsletter drove 1,000 visitors to his blog in 24 hours after featuring his customer service post. He reverse-engineers the referral chain from a librarian blog to the newsletter, and uses the unexpected traffic spike to illustrate two points: every software entrepreneur should have a blog, and non-technical professions like librarians represent viable, overlooked software markets.
key_points: A single link in a librarian's email newsletter drove 1,000 visitors in 24 hours, implying a readership in the high five to low six figures
Non-technical professional communities like librarians have their own thriving corners of the internet with large, engaged audiences
Software entrepreneurs systematically underestimate non-technical audiences because they project their own tech-centric worldview onto the market
Having a blog that could reach 1,000 potential customers is achievable and worth pursuing immediately
money_quotes: Given that literally one thousand readers have come to take a gander at that post over the last 24 hours, most attributable to that email newsletter, I would estimate that librarian's reach at probably in the high five to low six digits worth of extraordinarily dedicated readers.

Librarians, like school teachers, are quite smart folks who are not typically described as being on the leading edge of the technology curve. However, apparently there are an awful lot of them out there, and they have their own little pocket of cyberspace which is actually not little at all.
They are an addressable audience on the Internet, they read blogs at least part of the time, and they almost certainly have problems which can be solved by software.
Do you have a blog that 1,000 potential customers of your software could be reading tomorrow? If not, what exactly is keeping you from writing one?
Often uISVs have a mindset which is overly informed by being quite techy people, and they forget that the rest of the world uses computers, too.
Would you have written librarians off as a potential niche for a uISV, because they probably don't buy software anyhow?
key_insight: Software entrepreneurs miss huge markets by assuming non-technical professionals aren't viable customers, when in reality these communities are large, engaged, and reachable through their own thriving online networks.
